name: moon-upper-hemisphere
title: A Moment with the Moon
description: The Moon taken by the Artemis II crew. Shows Orientale basin in the center, with a black patch of ancient lava. The crater Byrgius and its distinctive rays are visible.
caption: Orientale basin and Byrgius crater rays

name: moon-nearside-maria
title: Keeper of the Night Sky
description: Just over half of the Moon's disk, with the near side's dark lava patches visible. Orientale basin appears encircled by mountain rings, Grimaldi crater shows as a dark spot, and Aristarchus crater is the bright white dot within a dark lava flow.
caption: Near side with Orientale, Grimaldi, and Aristarchus

name: moon-southern-craters-closeup
title: It's All in the Details
description: The rings of the Orientale basin, one of the Moon's youngest and best-preserved large impact craters. The concentric ring structures help scientists understand crater formation. Two smaller features — suggested names Integrity and Carroll — demonstrate how crew observations support real-time surface science.
caption: Orientale basin ring structures up close

name: earth-crescent-from-deep-space
title: Artemis Era Earthrise
description: Earth appears as a delicate crescent, with only its upper edge illuminated during the Artemis II lunar flyby. The lunar horizon is silhouetted against the illuminated Earth.
caption: Crescent Earth rising over the lunar horizon

name: moon-terminator-craters
title: Shadows at the Edge of Lunar Day
description: A portion of the Moon along the terminator — the boundary between lunar day and night — where low-angle sunlight casts long, dramatic shadows across the surface.
caption: Dramatic shadows along the day-night boundary

name: moon-central-crater-detail
title: Shadows Across Vavilov Crater
description: A close-up of Vavilov Crater on the rim of the older and larger Hertzsprung basin. The image captures the transition between smooth and rugged lunar terrain, with crater shadows highlighting the boundary between lunar day and night.
caption: Vavilov crater on the Hertzsprung basin rim

name: moon-terminator-surface
title: Ready for a Close Up
description: The heavily cratered terrain of the eastern edge of the South Pole-Aitken basin with the shadowed terminator.
caption: South Pole-Aitken basin at the terminator

name: moon-farside-with-earth
title: Earthset Over the Lunar Limb
description: Earth appears tiny as the Moon looms large in this photo taken during the lunar flyby.
caption: Tiny Earth beyond the cratered far side

name: earthrise-over-moon-limb
title: The Edge of Two Worlds
description: Earth draws closer to passing behind the Moon, about six minutes before Earthset. Earth is in a crescent phase, with sunlight coming from the right.
caption: Crescent Earth six minutes before Earthset

name: earth-above-lunar-horizon
title: A New View of the Moon
description: Earth sets at 6:41 p.m. EDT over the Moon's curved limb as the crew journeys around the far side of the Moon.
caption: Earthset over the lunar limb at 6:41 p.m.

name: earth-peeking-over-moon
title: A Setting Earth
description: The lunar surface in sharp detail with Earth setting in the background. Captured just three minutes before Orion and its crew went behind the Moon.
caption: Earth setting three minutes before loss of signal

name: moon-limb-distant-earth
title: Artemis II Total Solar Eclipse, Partial Frame
description: A total solar eclipse with only part of the Moon visible as it fully obscures the Sun. A glowing halo surrounds the dark lunar disk — possibly the corona, zodiacal light, or both. From deep space the Moon sustained nearly 54 minutes of totality. The bright glint on the left edge is Venus. Mare Crisium is visible along the lunar horizon.
caption: Total solar eclipse with Venus and Mare Crisium

name: solar-eclipse-diamond-ring
title: Solar Eclipse Emergence from Orion
description: The Sun beginning to peek out from behind the Moon as the eclipse transitions out of totality. A bright sliver of sunlight returns after nearly an hour of darkness, creating sharp contrast against the Moon's silhouette and revealing lunar topography along the limb.
caption: Sunlight returns after nearly an hour of totality

name: total-solar-eclipse-corona
title: Artemis II in Eclipse
description: The Moon fully eclipsing the Sun. From the crew's perspective, the Moon appeared large enough to create nearly 54 minutes of totality. A glowing halo surrounds the dark lunar disk. Stars normally too faint to see are visible, and the faint glow of the nearside is illuminated by Earthlight.
caption: 54-minute total eclipse with corona and Earthlight

name: moon-three-quarter-lit
title: Dark Craters, Bright Views
description: More than two-thirds of the Moon during the first observation shift, highlighting the 600-mile-wide Orientale basin along the boundary between the near and far sides, and Grimaldi crater with its dark mare lava floor and heavily worn rim.
caption: Orientale basin and Grimaldi crater from orbit

name: moon-farside-half-lit
title: The Edge of Darkness
description: The terminator boundary between day and night — described by the crew as 'anything but a straight line.' Crater rims stand out as 'islands' in the night. Giant crater chains from the 3.7-billion-year-old Orientale basin scour the surface of the even older Hertzsprung Basin.
caption: Crater chains from Orientale scarring Hertzsprung basin

name: moon-ancient-impact-basin
title: The Moon's Great Scar
description: A detailed image of the Orientale basin, a 600-mile-wide impact crater marked by a dark patch of ancient lava that erupted through the Moon's crust billions of years ago.
caption: Orientale basin with ancient lava flows

name: moon-limb-cratered-highlands
title: Craters of Time
description: Craters dotting the surface of the Moon, revealing its rugged, ancient surface. This scarred landscape reflects a long history of cosmic collisions.
caption: Rugged cratered highlands along the limb

name: moon-limb-solar-flare
title: A Solar Eclipse Like No Other
description: A wedge of the Moon in nighttime as the Sun sets on the opposite side, capturing the beginning of a total solar eclipse. Unlike minutes-long eclipses from Earth, the crew witnessed the Sun hide behind the Moon for nearly an hour. The bright rays running outward are the Sun's corona, normally hidden by surface light. The jagged lunar edge reveals backlit mountain topography.
caption: Solar corona streaming past the lunar limb

name: moon-nearside-bright-crater
title: A "Handprint" on the Moon
description: Contrasting dark and light features the crew compared to a handprint. From top to bottom: Oceanus Procellarum, Mare Humorum (the 'Sea of Moisture'), and the crater Byrgius A.
caption: Oceanus Procellarum, Mare Humorum, and Byrgius A

name: moon-southern-highlands
title: Ancient Lava on the Moon
description: A close-up of the near side as Orion approached for the flyby. The dark patches of ancient lava are visible on the top third. Aristarchus crater is the bright white dot in the midst of a dark grey lava flow.
caption: Near side with Aristarchus crater glowing bright

name: moon-mare-overhead
title: The Rings of the Orientale Basin
description: The rings of the Orientale basin, one of the Moon's youngest and best-preserved large impact craters, captured during the first observation shift.
caption: Orientale basin rings from the first observation shift

name: moon-terminator-shadow
title: Vavilov Crater Along the Hertzsprung Basin Rim
description: Hertzsprung Basin with its distinctive two concentric rings of mountains. Near the lower left, Vavilov crater — identified by its central peak — stands out, a feature often described by the crew during the flyby.
caption: Hertzsprung basin with Vavilov crater below

name: moon-limb-ray-craters
title: A Cross Section of Lunar Geology
description: A diverse set of lunar features including the bright Aristarchus crater, the Marius Hills volcanic domes, the sinuous Reiner Gamma swirl, rays from Glushko crater, and the dark-floored Grimaldi crater.
caption: Aristarchus, Marius Hills, and Reiner Gamma swirl

name: mare-imbrium-from-orbit
title: Seriously Moonstruck
description: The lower half of the Moon suspended in time. In the upper center, the Orientale basin with a black patch of ancient lava that punched through the crust in an eruption billions of years ago.
caption: Lower half of the Moon with Orientale basin

name: moon-dense-crater-field
title: A Crater of Remembrance
description: The small, bright spot in the center is the crater the crew proposed naming Carroll, after Commander Reid Wiseman's late wife. About 3.5 miles across, the proposed Carroll crater is on the nearside's western edge.
caption: Proposed Carroll crater, named for a loved one lost

name: moon-crescent-distant
title: A Moodier Moon
description: A shot from early in the flyby with a smaller aperture setting, showing a moodier version of the Moon. The crew spent about 7 hours photographing and recording observations as they flew around the far side.
caption: A moody crescent from early in the flyby

name: moon-full-disk
title: Capture My Good Side
description: More than two-thirds of the Moon showcasing the nearside's intricate features. The 600-mile-wide Orientale basin lies along the transition between near and far sides. The round black spot northeast is Grimaldi crater, known for its dark mare lava floor.
caption: Two-thirds of the Moon with Orientale and Grimaldi

name: moon-highlands-long-shadows
title: A Tapestry of Lunar Landmarks
description: Multiple lunar landmarks highlighted during the crew's observation call: Ohm crater, Oceanus Procellarum, Grimaldi crater, Pierazzo crater, the proposed Carroll crater, and the expansive Hertzsprung Basin — from dark volcanic plains to heavily cratered highlands.
caption: Ohm, Grimaldi, Pierazzo, and Carroll craters

name: moon-mare-through-window
title: Swoon at the Moon
description: The southwest portion of Orientale Basin highlighting its prominent annular ring — a sweeping arc of mountainous terrain formed by an ancient impact. Apollo-era observers nicknamed this formation 'the kiss' for its distinctive curved shape.
caption: Orientale basin annular ring, nicknamed the kiss

name: moon-through-orion-window
title: Sill Life
description: The Moon peeking above the window sill of the Orion spacecraft. The crew spent about 7 hours at the windows during the flyby, taking photos and recording observations to share with scientists.
caption: Lunar surface through the Orion window sill

name: moon-and-earth-from-orion
title: A Room With a View
description: A view from the Orion spacecraft window approximately 9 minutes before Earthset during the lunar flyby.
caption: Moon and Earth from Orion, 9 minutes before Earthset

name: orion-service-module
title: Guiding the Journey
description: The engines on the Orion service module from a camera on a solar array wing. The largest is the orbital maneuvering system engine, surrounded by eight smaller auxiliary thrusters.
caption: Service module engines from a solar array camera
